coredata.dc:title: Simultaneous detection of iodide and mercuric ions by nitrogen-sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots based on flow injection “turn off-on” chemiluminescence analysis system
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Junmei
Li, Yue
Han, Suqin
coredata.dc:description: A simple, rapid and sensitive nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots (NSGQD) based on flow injection chemiluminescence (FI-CL) technique for the determination of iodide and mercuric ions was reported. Strong CL signal was observed from the reaction of NSGQD and Ce(IV) under acidic condition. The mechanism was investigated by the spectra of CL, fluorescence and UV–vis. Moreover, the CL of the NSGQD-Ce(IV) system was found to be quenched by I−, and then recovered by Hg2+. In this way, a NSGQD-based CL “off-on” nanosensor for the simultaneous detection of I− and Hg2+ was designed. The “turn-off” mode was used for the determination of I− in a linear range of 0.04–3.0 μM with a detection limit of 4.23 nM. And the linear range for Hg2+ detection based on the “turn-on” process was from 0.3 to 7.0 μM with a 24.7 nM detection limit. The methods have been applied to the analysis of kelp, tea and water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: NSGQD
Turn-off
Turn-on
Ce(IV)
I−
Hg2+

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: UV–vis absorption, FL spectra, and XPS spectra of NSGQD
Directly oxidized CL and reaction mechanism
Construction of turn off-on system and sensing of I− and Hg2+
“Turn off” detection of I− in food samples
“Turn on” detection of Hg2+ in water samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the FI-CL system. P1: vice pump; P2: main pump; V: injection valve; F: CL flow-cell; PMT: photomultiplier tube; HV: negative high voltage, −600 V.

(A) FL intensity of NSGQD at different ratio between CA and L-cysteine. (B) UV–vis absorption spectrum and FL spectra of NSGQD. Insert: Photograph of NSGQD under natural light (left) and 365 nm UV light (right). (C) FL spectra of NSGQD under various pH. (D) XPS spectra of the NSGQD. (E–G) High-resolution C 1s, N 1sand S 2p peaks of the NSGQD.
(A) UV–vis absorption spectra. (B) FL spectra. (C) CL spectra. (D) CL kinetic curves.
The CL response of the NSGQD-Ce(IV) system and NSGQD-Ce(IV)-I− system to various ions (Xn±).
Comparison of the proposed I− detection method with other reported methods.
Results of I− and Hg2+ determination in real samples.

head.ce:title: Simultaneous detection of iodide and mercuric ions by nitrogen-sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots based on flow injection “turn off-on” chemiluminescence analysis system

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Material Science, Shanxi Normal University, Linfen 041000, Shanxi, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Material Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple, rapid and sensitive nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots (NSGQD) based on flow injection chemiluminescence (FI-CL) technique for the determination of iodide and mercuric ions was reported. Strong CL signal was observed from the reaction of NSGQD and Ce(IV) under acidic condition. The mechanism was investigated by the spectra of CL, fluorescence and UV–vis. Moreover, the CL of the NSGQD-Ce(IV) system was found to be quenched by I−, and then recovered by Hg2+. In this way, a NSGQD-based CL “off-on” nanosensor for the simultaneous detection of I− and Hg2+ was designed. The “turn-off” mode was used for the determination of I− in a linear range of 0.04–3.0 μM with a detection limit of 4.23 nM. And the linear range for Hg2+ detection based on the “turn-on” process was from 0.3 to 7.0 μM with a 24.7 nM detection limit. The methods have been applied to the analysis of kelp, tea and water samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Iodide (I−), an essential microelement of human, plays a pivotal role in mental development, neurological activity, thyroid function and so on. Deficiency and excess of I− in body can lead to several serious health problems such as thyroid diseases and mental retardation, which is a significant public health problem in many developing countries [1]. The World Health Organization (WHO) recommends iodine intake for adults is 150 μg per day [2]. Mercury (Hg2+) exposure poses a major threat to global ecosystems and human well-being in health respect. Even at low concentrations, it causes irreversible damage to human body in nervous systems, gingivitis, tumor formation etc. [3]. Therefore, these adverse effects cause interests in developing new approaches for fast, sensitive and selective detection of I− and Hg2+.

Over the last few years, nanomaterials, such as carbon nanomaterials [4], metal nanomaterials [5], semiconductor quantum dots [6], and nanocomposite [7–9] have been widely used for analytical applications. As a rising star of luminescent carbonaceous nanomaterial, graphene quantum dots (GQD) has attracted much attention recently. GQD is graphene sheets smaller than 100 nm with different shapes such as circular, ellipse, hexagon, parallelogram-type and rectangles [10]. GQD with few layers exhibits much stronger photoluminescence activity owing to their pronounced quantum confinement and edge effects. These exceptional properties and the high number of reactive sites make GQD powerful tool in analytical nanoscience and nanotechnology [4]. Quantum yield (QY) is an important factor for GQD and many researchers are concentrating on developing GQD with higher QY. Now various methods such as surface passivation or modification, element and inorganic salt doping have been used to enhance the QY of GQD. Among these methods, heteroatom doping approach is a simple and effective pathway to improve QY and to tune electrical, magnetic, and optical properties of GQD. Compared to surface functionalization and single-heteroatom doping, double elements doping can better improve poor optical characteristics of GQD. Especially, the QY of GQD is increased to >70% by double elements N and S doping [11]. In chemiluminescence (CL) systems, GQD can be employed as sensitizers and consequently can bring significant enhancement of some weak CL reactions benefiting from their size-dependent and optical properties. Therefore, CL sensors are extensively explored and developed based on the CL behavior of GQD [12].

“Turn off-on” detection mode can improve analytical selectivity and sensitivity, and realizes simultaneous determination of several components. At present, the switches mode has been used widely in the fluorescence (FL) analysis field [13–15]. In the CL system “turn off-on” mode was rarely employed and was only applied to the detection of one analyte that could recover the suppressed CL signal. For example, a “turn off-on” CL method for the determination of glutathione [16] or cyanide [17] was reported, which was based on the fact that glutathione or cyanide could recover the CL intensity of CdTe QDs-H2O2 system quenched by Cu2+.

In this work, we synthesized a strong and excitation-independent emission N and S double doping GQD (NSGQD) by using citric acid and L-cysteine as precursors, and its QY was as high as 85.6%. The strong CL signal was observed from the reaction between NSGQD and Ce(IV) under acidic condition, addition of trace amount of I− could cause significant CL quenching of the NSGQD-Ce(IV) system. In the presence of Hg2+, I− has a very high affinity with Hg2+, which resulted in the CL recovery of the NSGQD-based CL system. Thus, a novel, sensitive and selective NSGQD based on “turn off-on” CL system was established for the simultaneous determination of I− and Hg2+ in real samples.

Citric acid monohydrate (CA) was obtained from Xinxiang Chemical Reagents Factory (Henan, China). L-cysteine, potassium iodide and ammonium cerium (IV) sulfate were purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute (Tianjin, China). Mercuric chloride was purchased from Xi'an Chemical Reagents Factory (Shannxi, China). The chemicals used in experiments were analytical reagent grade. A 0.01 M stock solution of Ce(IV) was prepared by dissolving certain amount (NH4)2Ce(SO4)4 in 0.2 M H2SO4. The stock solutions of I− (1.0 mM) and Hg2+ (0.01 M) were prepared by dissolving moderate amount KI and HgCl2 solid in double distilled water, respectively. NSGQD was synthesized in our laboratory (seeing Supplementary material), and was diluted with water before use.

FL spectra were collected with an F-380 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tianjin port east science and technology development Limited by Share Ltd., Tianjin, China). UV–vis absorption spectra were performed using a TU-1810 PC UV–vis spectrophotometer (Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co., Ltd., Beijing, China). X-ray photo-electron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were conducted on a K-Alpha surface analysis system (Thermo, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image was recorded using a JEM-2100 electron microscopy (JEOL, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was obtained by Varian 660-IR spectrometer (Varian, USA). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) pattern was performed with an Ultima IV-185 X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan). CL spectra were collected by a series of high-energy cutoff filters. All CL experiments were carried out using a FI (flow injection)-CL system consisting of a BPCL ultra-weak luminescence analyzer (Institute of Biophysics, Chinese Academy of Science, Beijing, China) and an IFIS-C model intelligent FI sampler (Xi'an Remax Analytical Instrument Co. Ltd., Shannxi, China). The schematic diagrams of the FI-CL used in this work were shown in Fig. 1 , among “turn off” detection of I− was realized with route 1, and “turn off-on” detection of Hg2+ was carried out based on route 2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–vis absorption, FL spectra, and XPS spectra of NSGQD
ce:section.ce:para: NSGQD was synthetized with a facile pyrolysis route using CA and L-cysteine as C, N and S sources by ourselves. From Fig. 2A, the FL intensity of NSGQD was changed but the emission spectra was unchanged as the ratio of CA and L-cysteine increased. At the ratio of 1:0.5 between CA and L-cysteine, the maximum FL intensity of NSGQD was obtained, and the FL quantum yield (FLQY) was 85.6% using quinine sulfate as standard. Fig. 2B showed the UV–vis absorption spectrum and FL excitation and emission spectra of the as-prepared NSGQD in aqueous solution. A very bright blue luminescence of NSGQD under the illumination of UV (365 nm) light could be clearly seen in the inset of Fig. 2B. There was a absorption peak at 346 nm, corresponding to the n-π* transition of the CO bond. A strong FL emission peak occurred at 416 nm, and it was excitation-independent [18,19]. In other words, the change of excitation wavelength from 300 nm to 380 nm only led to the change of FL intensity. However, the FL intensity and peak position of NSGQD were affected by pH of solution. From Fig. 2C, the FL intensity of the NSGQD increased gradually and its emission spectra blue shifted as the pH increased from 1.0 to 5.0. When pH > 5, a strong and stable FL signal was obtained and its peak position remained unchanged [18].

XPS survey spectra of NSGQD (Fig. 2D) displayed four obvious peaks at ca. 531.9 eV, 400.2 eV, 284.7 eV, and 163.5 eV, which were attributed to O 1s, N 1s, C 1s and S 2p. As shown in the inset of Fig. 2C, the contents of four elements in the NSGQD were calculated to be 66.48% (C), 23.09% (O), 5.31% (N) and 5.12% (S), respectively, indicating the successful incorporation of N and S into the GQD. For the high-resolution C 1s region (Fig. 2E), there was three obvious characteristic peak at 288.3 eV, 285.6 eV, and 284.5 eV, corresponding to CO groups, C–N/C–O/C–S, and C=C/C–C in aromatic rings, respectively. The N 1s scan spectrum (Fig. 2F) revealed two peaks at 399.9 eV and 400.5 eV, which could be respectively corresponded to pyridinic type N and pyrrolic type N. The S 2p XPS spectra (Fig. 2G) demonstrated the presence of C-S-C units with the presence of two peaks at 163.3 eV (associated to S 2p 3/2) and 164.4 eV (associated to S 2p 1/2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Directly oxidized CL and reaction mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: The redox reactions between NSGQD and some common oxidants including Ce(IV), KMnO4, K2Cr2O7, K2S2O8, K3Fe(CN)6, H2O2, and KIO4 were investigated. The introduction of Ce (IV) into NSGQD resulted in CL with highest intensity, other oxidants produced a very weak or not CL signal. So Ce(IV) was selected as oxidant for the CL reaction. The main reason may be that the carboxyl exists on the surface of NSGQD [20], and the chemical energy generated during the reaction of NSGQD with Ce(IV) is more matched with the excitation energy required for the formation the excited-state NSGQD than the other oxidants.

Fig. 3A showed that in the NSGQD-Ce(IV) system, the absorption band around 320 nm of Ce(IV) disappeared and the absorption peak of NSGQD had red shift from 346 nm to 360 nm. Meanwhile, the FL spectrum of NSGQD only presented a decreased intensity after adding Ce(IV) (Fig. 3B). These showed that a reaction had taken place between NSGQD and Ce(IV). Further, the CL spectrum of the NSGQD-Ce(IV) system showed a maximum wavelength of around 505 nm (Fig. 3C), which was substantially red-shifted from that in the FL spectra of NSGQD. Maybe it was because CL depended on surface chemistry and the presence of surface states, and FL mainly occurred through excitation and emission within the NSGQD core. The energy separation of the NSGQD surface states was smaller than the band gaps of the NSGQD core [21,22]. So, the emitter in NSGQD-Ce(IV) system was excited-state NSGQD (NSGQD*).

NSGQD has large surface area and charge injection probably occur via its surface states. Ce(IV), as a oxidants, could inject holes into the NSGQD [23] and convert NSGQD to positively charged NSGQD (NSGQD +) (reaction 1). Meanwhile, the excitation of electrons to high energy levels of the NSGQD at a temperature above absolute zero could produce negatively charged NSGQD (NSGQD −) (reaction 2) [24]. The NSGQD + and NSGQD − via electron-transfer annihilation processes formed NSGQD* (reaction 3). When NSGQD* returned to the ground state accompanying with photon irradiation, the CL emission was produced (reaction 4).
(1)
NSGQD + Ce
IV
→ NSGQD ˙
+
+ Ce
III
(2)
NSGQD + e
thermal excitation
→ NSGQD ˙
−
(3)
NSGQD ˙
+
+ NSGQD ˙
−
→
NSGQD ∗
(4)
NSGQD ∗
→ NSGQD + hν

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of turn off-on system and sensing of I− and Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: As discussed above, Ce(IV) was able to directly elicit a stronger CL from NSGQD in acidic medium. In our preliminary experiment, the CL intensity of the NSGQD-Ce(IV) system was first quenched by I− and then recovered by Hg2+. So, the simultaneous detection of I− and Hg2+ was proposed based on the “turn off-on” detection system.

The UV–vis spectra showed that the absorbance of Ce(IV) at 320 nm was greatly reduced and I− at 226 nm disappeared (Fig. 3A), which indicated a redox happened between Ce(IV) and I−. In this way, the CL intensity of the NSGQD-Ce(IV) system was diminished by I−. On the other hand, Hg2+ could enhance the CL of NSGQD-Ce(IV)-I− system due to the stronger binding between Hg2+ and I− [25], and leading to the formation of HgI2 or [HgI4]2−. So the inhibitory CL was recovered.

The dynamic CL intensity-time profiles acquired by static-injection mode indicated that these CL reactions of the NSGQD-Ce(IV) system, NSGQD-Ce(IV)-I− system, and NSGQD-Ce(IV)-I−-Hg2+ system were very quick and their CL intensity reached a maximum for 1 s after initiating the reactions. And then the quenching effect of I− and the resilience of Hg2+ for the corresponding CL systems were also shown in Fig. 3D.

ce:section.ce:section-title: “Turn off” detection of I− in food samples
ce:section.ce:para: In Fig. 4 , the CL response of the NSGQD-Ce(IV) system was studied in the presence of different ions at the concentration of 1.0 μM. It was noticeable that only I− could induce a serious decrease of the CL intensity, implying that this CL system could be used to selectively detect I−. The experimental parameters were optimized to obtain the maximum sensitivity for detection of I− (Fig. S2), and the results were as follows: 0.16 mM Ce(IV) in 0.02 M H2SO4 medium, 1:200 dilution ratio of NSGQD with water, 2.7 mL·min−1 flow rate of main pump. The ΔI (difference of NSGQD-Ce(IV) in the absence and presence of I−) was linearly proportional to the logarithm of I− concentrations in the range from 0.04 to 3.0 μM (Fig. S3A and B). The regression equation was ΔI = 90,315 + 64,278 lg[I−] (μM), with the corresponding regression coefficient of 0.9952. The detection limit (LOD) was as low as 4.23 nM based on threefold of standard deviation. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was 0.84% for eleven replicated determination of 0.2 μM I−. These results indicated that the precision and accuracy were good enough for the detection of I− at a low level. Table 1 compared the sensitivity of our method and other reported methods [25–31] for the detection of I−. The LOD of our method was comparable or lower than that of others, and the linear range was also relatively wide, demonstrating it was a promising method for the detection of I−.

The established detection system was applied to determine I− in kelp and tea samples. As seen from Fig. 4, coexisting ions like Hg2+ and Ag+ would interfere the CL quenching by I−. In order to eliminate the possible interference, the ashed sample solution was first treated by sodium sulfide to remove them. The content of I− in kelp and tea samples by our selected was 1.79 mg·g−1 and 0.64 μg·g−1, respectively. The recovery was in the range of 99.0–106.3%, with the RSD ≤ 4.96% (Table 2 ), proving the accuracy and reliability of this assay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: “Turn on” detection of Hg2+ in water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The recovery property of Hg2+ for the CL signal of NSGQD-Ce(IV)-I− system had also been investigated by adding same concentrations of ions (10.0 μM) to the mixed NSGQD-Ce(IV)-I− solution. As shown in Fig. 4, when Hg2+ was added, the recovery rate of CL signal reached 92.5%, and Ag+ could recover 35.6%, no obvious signal changes were observed for other substances. The Ag+ could be removed by adding sodium chloride solution. So, the high selectivity of this method provided a potential for the efficient detection of Hg2+. The CL variation (ΔI, the difference of NS-GQD-Ce(IV)-I− in the presence and absence of Hg2+) with the concentrations of Hg2+ was linear in the range of 0.3–7.0 μM, and a LOD (3σ) was 0.02 μM (Fig. S3C and D). The linear equation was ΔI = 18,049 [Hg2+] (μM) – 9,323 (r = 0.9962). The RSD was 0.92% for eleven determinations of 2.0 μM Hg2+. The proposed CL method was compared with other reported nanomaterial-based optical methods [25,32–36] as shown in Table S2. The detection limit was comparable to those of previous reports for Hg2+ detection. The practical application of the “turn-on” CL method for the determination of Hg2+ in water samples was investigated. Hg2+ was not detected in water samples. The results were exhibited in Table 2. The recovery ranged from 97.0 to 106.0%. The results revealed that the method for determination Hg2+ in water samples was reliable and feasible.

The possible interference from other metal ions in the CL response to Hg2+ was investigated. The tolerable concentration ratios for interferences were taken as in relative error of <5%. Tolerance content ratios for the determination of 2.0 μM Hg2+ were over 1000-fold over CH3COO−, SO4 2−, NO3 −; 500-fold for Cl−, Co2+, Cd2+, K+, Ba2+, Sn2+; 250-fold for Fe3+, Ni2+; 100-fold for F−, Br−, NO2 −, Pb2+, Cr3+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Al3+; 10-fold for Fe2+; 2-fold for Ag+. The species which might be in water samples had no effect on the Hg2+ determination. These results suggested that this CL system was feasible for Hg2+ detecting.

In summary, NSGQD exhibited the strong CL ability in the presence of acidified Ce(IV). The CL intensity of the NSGQD-Ce(IV) system was markedly quenched by I−, which was ascribed to the reaction between I− and Ce(IV), resulting in the consumption of Ce(IV). Furthermore, the Hg2+ could restore the quenched CL signal because of the strong combination of Hg2+ with I−. Thus we designed a “turn off-on” CL analysis method based on the NSGQD-Ce(IV) system for I− and Hg2+ detection with high sensitivity and outstanding selectivity.
There are no conflicts of interest to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province (Grant No. 2013011013–3), and the fundamental research fund of Modern College of Humanities and Sciences of Shanxi Normal University (2018JCYJ12).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2019.04.039.
